grammar: no object, with adverbial of direction
with object and adverbial of direction

senses.definition: move at a speed faster than a walk, never having both or all the feet on the ground at the same time
examples: the dog ran across the road
she ran the last few yards, breathing heavily
he hasn't paid for his drinks—run and catch him

senses.definition: run as a sport or for exercise
examples: I run every morning

senses.definition: compete in a race
examples: she ran in the 200 meters
: Dave has run 42 marathons

senses.definition: enter (a racehorse) for a race.
move about in a hurried and hectic way
examples: I've spent the whole day running around after the kids

senses.definition: run from one wicket to the other in scoring or attempting to score a run.
attempt to advance to the next base.
chase (someone) away
examples: ah went tuh eat the mangoes but the people run mih

senses.definition: chase or hunt their quarry
examples: the hounds are running

senses.definition: sail directly before the wind, especially in bad weather
examples: we slanted across to the far bank and ran before the wind

senses.definition: go upriver from the sea in order to spawn.
pass or cause to pass quickly or smoothly in a particular direction
examples: : the rumor ran through the pack of photographers
: Helen ran her fingers through her hair
registers: no object, with adverbial of direction
with object and adverbial of direction

senses.definition: move or cause to move somewhere forcefully or with a particular result
examples: : the tanker ran aground off the Aleutian Islands
: a woman ran a stroller into the back of my legs

senses.definition: fail to stop at (a red traffic light)
examples: cameras triggered by cars running red lights at intersections

senses.definition: navigate (rapids or a waterfall) in a boat
examples: the boats were preparing to run the big rapids

senses.definition: flow or cause to flow
examples: : a small river runs into the sea at one side of the castle
: she ran cold water into the sink
registers: no object, with adverbial of direction

senses.definition: cause water to flow over (something)
examples: I ran my hands under the faucet

senses.definition: fill (a bath) with water
examples: she ran a bath and lowered herself into the water
: I'll run you a nice hot bath

senses.definition: emit or exude a liquid
examples: she was weeping loudly, and her nose was running

senses.definition: melt and become fluid
examples: it was so hot that the butter ran

senses.definition: rise higher or flow more quickly
examples: there was still a heavy sea running
senses.semanticHint: (of the sea, the tide, or a river)

senses.definition: dissolve and spread when the fabric or paper becomes wet
examples: the red dye ran when the socks were washed
senses.semanticHint: (of dye or color in fabric or paper)

senses.definition: extend or cause to extend in a particular direction
examples: : cobbled streets run down to a tiny harbor
: he ran a wire under the carpet
registers: no object, with adverbial of direction
with object and adverbial of direction

senses.definition: pass into or reach a specified state or level
examples: inflation is running at 11 percent
: the decision ran counter to previous government commitments

senses.definition: make a regular journey on a particular route
examples: buses run into town every half hour
senses.semanticHint: (of a bus, train, ferry, or other form of transportation)

senses.definition: put (a particular form of public transportation) in service
examples: the group is drawing up plans to run trains on key routes

senses.definition: take (someone) somewhere in a car
examples: I'll run you home
registers: with object and adverbial of direction

senses.definition: be in charge of; manage
examples: Andrea runs her own catering business
: an attractive family-run hotel

senses.definition: operate or proceed in a particular way
examples: everything's running according to plan

senses.definition: organize and make available for other people
examples: we decided to run a series of seminars

senses.definition: own, maintain, and use (a vehicle)
examples: he could no longer afford to run a car

senses.definition: be in or cause to be in operation; function or cause to function
examples: : the car runs on unleaded fuel
: a number of peripherals can be run off one SCSI port

senses.definition: move or cause to move between the spools of a recording machine
examples: : I ran the tape back
: the tape has run out

senses.definition: continue or be valid or operative for a particular period of time
examples: the course ran for two days
this particular debate will run on and on

senses.definition: happen or arrive at the specified time
examples: the program was running fifteen minutes late

senses.definition: be staged or presented
examples: the play ran on Broadway last year

senses.definition: be a candidate in a political election
examples: he announced that he intended to run for President

senses.definition: sponsor (a candidate) in an election
examples: they ran their first candidate for the school board

senses.definition: publish or be published in a newspaper or magazine
examples: : the tabloids ran the story
: when the story ran, there was a big to-do

senses.definition: have a specified wording or contents
examples: “Tapestries slashed!” ran the dramatic headline
senses.semanticHint: (of a story, argument, or piece of writing)

senses.definition: bring (goods) into a country illegally and secretly; smuggle
examples: they run drugs for the cocaine cartels

senses.definition: cost (someone) (a specified amount)
examples: a new photocopier will run us about $1,300

senses.definition: develop a vertical line of unraveled stitches.
senses.semanticHint: (of a stocking or pair of tights)

senses.definition: an act or spell of running
examples: I usually go for a run in the morning
a cross-country run

senses.definition: a running pace
examples: Bobby set off at a run

senses.definition: an annual mass migration of fish up a river to spawn, or their return migration afterward
examples: the annual salmon runs

senses.definition: a journey accomplished or route taken by a vehicle, aircraft, or boat, especially on a regular basis
examples: the New York-Washington run

senses.definition: a short excursion made in a car
examples: we could take a run out to the country

senses.definition: the distance covered in a specified period, especially by a ship
examples: a record run of 398 miles from noon to noon

senses.definition: a short flight made by an aircraft on a straight and even course at a constant speed before or while dropping bombs
examples: bombing runs by B52s

senses.definition: an opportunity or attempt to achieve something
examples: their absence means the Russians will have a clear run at the title

senses.definition: a preliminary test of the efficiency of a procedure or system
examples: if you are styling your hair yourself, have a practice run

senses.definition: an attempt to secure election to political office
examples: his run for the Republican nomination

senses.definition: a continuous spell of a particular situation or condition
examples: he's had a run of bad luck

senses.definition: a continuous series of performances
examples: the play had a long run on Broadway

senses.definition: a quantity or amount of something produced at one time
examples: a production run of only 150 cars

senses.definition: a continuous stretch or length of something
examples: long runs of copper piping

senses.definition: a rapid series of musical notes forming a scale.
a sequence of cards of the same suit.
a widespread and sudden or continuous demand for (a particular currency or commodity)
examples: there's been a big run on nostalgia toys this year

senses.definition: a sudden demand for repayment from (a bank) made by a large number of lenders
examples: growing nervousness among investors led to a run on some banks

senses.definition: the average or usual type of person or thing
examples: she stood out from the general run of varsity cheerleaders

senses.definition: a sloping snow-covered course or track used for skiing, bobsledding, or tobogganing
examples: a ski run

senses.definition: a track made or regularly used by a particular animal
examples: a badger run

senses.definition: an enclosed area in which domestic animals or birds can run freely in the open
examples: a chicken run

senses.definition: a large open stretch of land used for pasture or the raising of stock
examples: one of the richest cattle runs of the district

senses.definition: free and unrestricted use of or access to
examples: her cats were given the run of the house

senses.definition: a point scored when a base runner reaches home plate after touching the other bases.
a point scored by hitting the ball so that both batters are able to run between the wickets, or awarded in some other circumstances.
a vertical line of unraveled stitches in stockings or tights
examples: she had a run in her nylons

senses.definition: a downward trickle of paint or a similar substance when applied too thickly
examples: varnish should be applied with care to avoid runs and an uneven surface

senses.definition: a small stream
examples: a shallow run at the edge of a low rock

senses.definition: diarrhea.
the after part of a ship's bottom where it rises and narrows toward the stern.

notes.content: On the use of verbs used with and instead of a to infinitive, as in ‘run and fetch the paper’, see and

Old English rinnan, irnan (verb), of Germanic origin, probably reinforced in Middle English by Old Norse rinna, renna. The current form with -u- in the present tense is first recorded in the 16th century.

items.definition: be eager to do what someone wants
examples: he had only to snap his fingers, and she would come running

items.definition: trying to avoid being captured
examples: a kidnapper on the run from the FBI
he took a pass on the run
I'm on the run every minute of the day

items.definition: (of a well or river) cease to flow or have any water.
examples: municipal relief funds had long since run dry

items.definition: carry out an errand for someone.
talk excessively or indiscreetly
examples: he just keeps running his mouth until I get really angry

items.definition: force someone to leave a place
examples: one of the big goals of the program was running the drug dealers out of town

items.definition: dominate or be in charge of a project, undertaking, or domain
examples: you're running the show—what do we do now?

items.definition: be suffering from a fever or high temperature.
fall into disrepair; gradually deteriorate.
meet or find someone by chance
examples: I just thought you might have run across him before

items.definition: go away (used typically to address a child)
examples: run along now, there's a good girl

items.definition: associate habitually with someone
examples: he's a good lad, but he started running around with the wrong bunch

items.definition: rush toward someone to attack them or as if to attack them
examples: she ran at him, kicking him with all her force

items.definition: leave or escape from a place, person, or situation
examples: children who run away from home normally go to big cities
Fran, let's run away together
he ran away with his sister-in-law
the commissioners are running away from their responsibilities

items.definition: (of one's imagination or emotions) escape one's control
examples: Susan's imagination was running away with her
a lot of people ran away with the idea that they were pacifists
the Yankees ran away with the series

items.definition: (of a vehicle) hit a person or animal and knock them to the ground
examples: the boy was run down by joyriders
it looks like the ferry captain deliberately ran down the yacht
you mustn't keep running yourself down
she finally ran the professor down
hardwood stocks in some countries are rapidly running down
he has criticized the government for running down the Armed Forces
the battery has run down
switching the hearing aid on and off ran the batteries down
the property had been allowed to run down
the former owners ran the place down

items.definition: (of a quality, trait, or condition) be common or inherent in members of a family, especially over several generations
examples: weight problems run in my family
I'm going to have to run you in
whatever system you choose, you must run it in properly
you need to run the engine in gently for the first 1,000 miles

items.definition: collide with someone or something
examples: he ran into a lamppost
I ran into Moira on the way home
the bank ran into financial difficulties
the company had debts running into millions of dollars
her words ran into each other

items.definition: leave a place or situation suddenly
examples: he ran off to join Napoleon's army and never returned
he proposed to me and we were running off together
he ran off with his wife's best friend
the sweat ran off their faces
run off the water that has been standing in the pipes
please run off some copies of that report
I ran off an article on the history of the Christmas tree

items.definition: steal something
examples: the treasurer had run off with the pension funds

items.definition: continue without stopping; go on longer than is expected
examples: the story ran on for months
you're right, I do run on a bit at times, sorry
my thoughts always ran too much on death

items.definition: (of a supply of something) be used up
examples: our food is about to run out
we've run out of gasoline
her contract runs out at the end of the year
slowly, he let the cables run out
a row of buildings ran out to Cityline Avenue

items.definition: abandon someone
examples: it seems Jack's run out on her and the three children

items.definition: (of a vehicle) knock a person or animal down and pass over their body
examples: I almost ran over that raccoon
the bath's running over
the filming ran over schedule and budget
her mind ran over their previous conversation

items.definition: be present in every part of something; pervade
examples: a sense of personal loss runs through many of his lyrics
her husband had long since run through her money
I'll just run through the schedule for the weekend
okay, let's run through Scene 3 again
Campbell threatened to run him through with his sword

items.definition: extend to or reach a specified amount or size
examples: the document ran to almost 100 pages
my income doesn't run to luxuries like taxis
he was tall and running to fat
don't come running to me for a handout

items.definition: allow a debt or bill to accumulate
examples: he ran up debts of $153,000
North Carolina ran up a 62–44 lead
I'll run up a dress for you
they ran up the star and crescent

items.definition: experience or meet a difficulty or problem
examples: the proposal has been dropped because it could run up against Federal regulations

items.definition: be covered or streaming with a liquid
examples: his face was running with sweat
we do lots of tests before we run with a product
ever since Lou dropped out, he's been running with a bad crowd
